name: REPLACE-ME: Example Grammar
description: REPLACE-ME: one or two sentences describing what this grammar is (a deck, a book, a sequence, an interpretation set) and where its content comes from. This file is the worked example the recursive-starter template ships with — copy this folder's SHAPE for every real grammar you author, then delete this one (or leave it as a live reference of the schema).
creator_link: REPLACE-ME: your website or profile URL

attribution.name: REPLACE-ME: source name (a book, a public-domain text, an original composition)

sections.Description: REPLACE-ME: the main prose for this item — what it is, what it means, what it evokes. This is the section every viewer renders first.
sections.Notes: REPLACE-ME: a second section — e.g. historical context, a citation, a usage note. Add as many named sections as your grammar needs; section NAMES are yours to choose (Description/Notes here are just an example pairing).

metadata.REPLACE-ME-field: Use metadata for small structured facts a viewer might filter or badge on (a date, an element, a numeric rank) — see GRAMMAR_FORMAT.md for reserved keys like source_deck/source_item_id (cross-grammar link pills) and youtube_video_id (performance embeds).

sections.Description: REPLACE-ME: every item in items[] follows the same shape — id, name, category, sections. Nothing else is required.
sections.Notes: REPLACE-ME: this second item shares the same section names as the first (Description/Notes) — that's a convention, not a requirement, but viewers read more cleanly when sibling items share section names.

sections.What this groups: REPLACE-ME: a composite item has NO image of its own — it groups other items via composite_of (an array of their ids). This is how a second-level structure (a suit, a chapter, a family of hexagrams, a historiographical pattern) emerges without a database. See GRAMMAR_FORMAT.md 'Composite items' for the full contract — there is no separate top-level 'emergences' array; composites live in items[] just like leaves.
